examples.title: Peterson's Solution Pseudocode
examples.description: Software solution for critical section problem for two processes
examples.explanation: Process sets its flag to indicate desire to enter, then gives priority to the other process. It waits in the while loop until the other process is not interested or it is its turn. Provides mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting for two processes.

examples.title: Semaphore Implementation (Pseudo-C-like)
examples.code:
// Atomic hardware instruction
FUNCTION TestAndSet(VAR lock : BOOLEAN) : BOOLEAN
  ATOMIC BEGIN
    old := lock
    lock := TRUE
    RETURN old
  END

// Mutex using spinlock
CLASS Semaphore
  available : BOOLEAN := FALSE
  
  PROCEDURE wait()
    WHILE TestAndSet(available) = TRUE DO
      // busy wait - spinlock
    END WHILE
    // lock acquired
  
  PROCEDURE signal()
    available := FALSE
    // lock released

// Counting Semaphore with queue
CLASS CountingSemaphore
  count : INTEGER := initialValue
  queue : FIFO_QUEUE
  
  PROCEDURE wait()
    count := count - 1
    IF count < 0 THEN
      queue.add(currentProcess)
      block currentProcess
    END IF
  
  PROCEDURE signal()
    count := count + 1
    IF count <= 0 THEN
      proc := queue.remove()
      wakeup proc
    END IF
examples.explanation: Shows both spinlock-based binary semaphore and proper counting semaphore with blocking. TestAndSet provides atomic read-modify-write operation. The counting version blocks processes when count goes negative.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for binary, O(n) for queue operations

examples.title: Producer-Consumer Problem Solution
examples.description: Solution to bounded buffer problem using semaphores in pseudocode
examples.code:
CONSTANTS:
  BUFFER_SIZE = n

SHARED VARIABLES:
  buffer[0..n-1] : ARRAY
  in, out : INTEGER := 0
  
  // Semaphores
  empty : CountingSemaphore := BUFFER_SIZE  // empty slots
  full : CountingSemaphore := 0             // filled slots  
  mutex : BinarySemaphore := 1              // mutual exclusion

// Producer Process
PROCEDURE producer()
  WHILE TRUE DO
    item := produce()
    empty.wait()          // decrement empty count
    mutex.wait()          // enter critical section
    buffer[in] := item
    in := (in + 1) MOD BUFFER_SIZE
    mutex.signal()        // exit critical section
    full.signal()          // increment filled count
  END WHILE

// Consumer Process
PROCEDURE consumer()
  WHILE TRUE DO
    full.wait()           // decrement filled count
    mutex.wait()          // enter critical section
    item := buffer[out]
    out := (out + 1) MOD BUFFER_SIZE
    mutex.signal()        // exit critical section
    empty.signal()        // increment empty count
    consume(item)
  END WHILE
examples.explanation: The solution uses three semaphores: empty counts available slots (initialized to buffer size), full counts filled slots (initialized to 0), and mutex ensures mutual exclusion. Producer waits for empty slot, adds item, signals full. Consumer waits for full slot, removes item, signals empty.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for buffer plus semaphore overhead

examples.title: Banker's Algorithm - Safety Check
examples.description: Algorithm to check if system is in safe state
examples.code:
// Data structures
available[1..m]    // available resources
maximum[1..n][1..m]  // maximum demand of each process
allocation[1..n][1..m]  // current allocation
need[1..n][1..m]     // remaining need = maximum - allocation

// Safety Algorithm
FUNCTION isSafe() : BOOLEAN
  work[1..m] := available      // temporary work vector
  finish[1..n] := FALSE        // all processes unfinished
  
  // Find process that can finish
  REPEAT
    found := FALSE
    FOR i := 1 TO n DO
      IF finish[i] = FALSE AND need[i] <= work THEN
        // Process can finish
        work := work + allocation[i]
        finish[i] := TRUE
        found := TRUE
      END IF
    END FOR
  UNTIL found = FALSE
  
  // Check if all processes can finish
  FOR i := 1 TO n DO
    IF finish[i] = FALSE THEN
      RETURN FALSE  // unsafe state
    END IF
  END FOR
  RETURN TRUE  // safe state

// Resource Request Algorithm
FUNCTION requestResources(process, request[1..m]) : BOOLEAN
  IF request > need[process] THEN
    RETURN ERROR  // exceeded maximum
  END IF
  
  IF request > available THEN
    RETURN FALSE  // must wait
  END IF
  
  // Pretend to allocate
  available := available - request
  allocation[process] := allocation[process] + request
  need[process] := need[process] - request
  
  // Check safety
  IF isSafe() THEN
    RETURN TRUE  // request granted
  ELSE
    // Rollback
    available := available + request
    allocation[process] := allocation[process] - request
    need[process] := need[process] + request
    RETURN FALSE  // request denied
  END IF
examples.explanation: The safety algorithm finds a sequence where all processes can complete by repeatedly finding processes whose needs can be satisfied with current work. If all finish, state is safe. Resource request algorithm pretends to allocate, checks safety, and rolls back if unsafe.
